name: World War II - European/African/Atlantic Theater
summary: The European/African/Atlantic Theater of World War II ran from Germany's invasion of Poland in September 1939 to Germany's surrender in May 1945. It included conventional war, strategic bombing, occupation terror, genocide, siege starvation, forced labor, mass deportation, and the Battle of the Atlantic. The theater caused roughly 39–45 million deaths, with Axis—especially Nazi German—policies responsible for the overwhelming majority of civilian deaths.

military_killed.notes: Approximate European/African/Atlantic Axis military deaths. Germany dominates; estimates for German military dead range from about 5.3 million in Overmans to 5.533 million in the National WWII Museum table, with Italy, Romania, Hungary, Finland, Bulgaria, and smaller Axis partners adding roughly 0.8–1.3 million depending on definitions.

breakdown.cause: mass shooting and mobile killing operations
breakdown.victims_group: Jews, Roma, Soviet civilians, communist officials, and others in occupied eastern Europe
breakdown.notes: Einsatzgruppen and associated mobile killing operations killed more than 1.4 million people, mostly Jews. This overlaps with the six million Jewish Holocaust total and with Soviet/Polish national death totals.
Russian-source civilian-loss accounting reports 7.42 million Soviet civilians intentionally killed under German occupation. This figure overlaps with Jewish victims and other categories.

Common scholarly/USHMM-linked summaries estimate about 1.8–1.9 million non-Jewish Polish civilians killed by German occupation policies and war. This is separate from roughly 3 million Polish Jews murdered in the Holocaust.

breakdown.victims_group: British, French, Soviet, Dutch, Belgian, Yugoslav, Greek, and other civilians
breakdown.notes: Axis aerial bombardment and shelling killed civilians across Europe, including about 60,595 in Britain, 53,601 in France, and 51,000 in the USSR in Richard Overy's bombing-war synthesis.

civilians_killed_directly.notes: Not arithmetically summable because Jewish, Soviet, Polish, Roma, and other victims overlap across national, ethnic, and method-of-killing categories.

deaths_from_actions.notes: Canonical Holocaust total for Jews murdered by Nazi Germany and collaborators; includes shootings, killing centers, camps, ghettos, starvation, disease, forced labor, and death marches. About 2.7 million Jews were murdered in killing centers.
Soviet POWs in German captivity died from deliberate starvation, exposure, executions, disease, and forced labor; common estimates are 2.8–3.3 million deaths out of about 5.7 million captured.

Includes Russian-source categories of 2.164 million forced-labor deaths in Germany and 4.1 million famine/disease deaths in occupied territory, plus uncertainty around siege and occupation mortality. The Siege of Leningrad alone caused at least 750,000 and commonly about 800,000–1,000,000+ civilian deaths.
USHMM gives 250,000–500,000 Roma murdered; other museum summaries state over 250,000.

deaths_from_actions.victims_group: people with physical and mental disabilities
deaths_from_actions.notes: USHMM lists about 250,000 people with disabilities murdered by Nazi Germany and collaborators.

military_killed.notes: Approximate Allied military deaths in the European/African/Atlantic theater. Soviet losses dominate: National WWII Museum gives 8.8–10.7 million Soviet military deaths, while Russian official figures give 8,668,400 Soviet armed-forces losses. Other Allied military losses include Polish, Yugoslav, British/Commonwealth, French, Greek, U.S. European/Mediterranean, and smaller Allied forces.

breakdown.victims_group: German civilians and civilians in German-occupied or Axis Europe
breakdown.notes: USSBS gave a minimum of 305,000 killed by air attack in Germany; Overy gives 353,000 bombing deaths in Germany and additional deaths in France, Italy, occupied Europe, and elsewhere.
Katyn massacre: IPN's minimum estimate is 21,768 victims; general descriptions often round to almost 22,000.

breakdown.victims_group: political prisoners in Soviet-annexed eastern Europe
breakdown.notes: NKVD prisoner massacres in 1941 are estimated by location; some summaries give an approximate total up to 100,000 extrajudicial executions over several weeks.

breakdown.victims_group: German and other civilians during the Red Army advance and occupation
breakdown.notes: Direct violent-death component is difficult to isolate from broader German flight/expulsion mortality. German Federal Archive-derived discussions cite about 100,000–120,000 civilians killed during flight/evacuation and higher broader categories when camps, forced labor, hunger, and disease are included.

civilians_killed_directly.notes: Includes direct civilian killing by Allied bombing and Soviet security/military violence. Does not include ordinary battlefield collateral deaths where perpetrator cannot be reliably identified.

deaths_from_actions.victims_group: German civilians and ethnic Germans from eastern/central Europe
deaths_from_actions.notes: Highly contested. Modern lower estimates often emphasize about 500,000–600,000 confirmed/supportable deaths; older demographic estimates and Britannica-style summaries cite 2.384 million German deaths during 1944–46 from Soviet invasion and forced transfers.

Soviet archival-based figures list 580,589 Axis POW deaths in Soviet captivity, including 381,067 Germans. Some Western/German estimates for German POW deaths in Soviet custody alone approach 1.0–1.1 million.

deaths_from_actions.victims_group: Soviet citizens, annexed populations, deportees, and prisoners
deaths_from_actions.notes: Soviet archival figures give 621,637 Gulag prisoner deaths in 1941–45; other Russian-source/Applebaum-cited totals give 932,000 deaths. These deaths are policy-caused but not all are directly linked to battlefield operations.

IPN-linked summaries report roughly 1.0 million Polish citizens arrested, conscripted, or deported by Soviet occupiers in 1939–41, including 475,000 deported to remote USSR regions, but deaths cannot be cleanly separated without double-counting Gulag/deportation mortality.

totals.notes: The grand total is a theater-level approximation. Side-caused death categories cannot be summed directly because Holocaust, national, camp, POW, Soviet, Polish, and expulsion categories overlap. The lower bound reflects a European-theater total of at least about 39 million; the upper bound allows for higher Soviet, German expulsion, famine/disease, and uncertain civilian categories while excluding the Asia-Pacific theater.

key_atrocities.name: The Holocaust
key_atrocities.notes: Nazi Germany and collaborators murdered six million Jews through shootings, killing centers, ghettos, camps, starvation, disease, forced labor, and death marches.

key_atrocities.name: Nazi murder of Soviet POWs
key_atrocities.notes: Soviet POWs died in German captivity from starvation, exposure, shootings, disease, and forced labor.

key_atrocities.name: Siege of Leningrad
key_atrocities.notes: Civilian deaths were caused mainly by starvation, cold, disease, bombardment, and siege conditions.

key_atrocities.name: Katyn massacre
key_atrocities.victims_group: Polish POWs, police, officials, and prisoners
key_atrocities.notes: Soviet NKVD executions of Polish prisoners following the Soviet invasion and occupation of eastern Poland.

key_atrocities.name: Wola massacre
key_atrocities.notes: German and collaborationist forces systematically killed civilians during suppression of the Warsaw Uprising; some Polish estimates are higher.

key_atrocities.name: Bombing of Dresden
key_atrocities.notes: Britannica gives 25,000–35,000; a German official commission concluded up to about 25,000 deaths.

key_atrocities.name: Operation Gomorrah / Bombing of Hamburg
key_atrocities.notes: The Hamburg firestorm killed tens of thousands; common figures range from about 34,000 to 46,000.

uncertainty_notes: Major uncertainties include theater boundaries; overlap between ethnicity, nationality, and place-of-death categories; inconsistent treatment of POWs and missing persons; whether postwar expulsion deaths through 1946 or 1950 are included; and the political sensitivity of Soviet, German, Polish, and Holocaust-related casualty accounting. The JSON estimates should be read as source-supported ranges, not exact counts.
